text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Breast Medical Oncology — Established Patient Visit

PATIENT: Denise Okafor
AGE: 50 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IIB (T2N1M0) Triple-Negative Invasive Ductal Carcinoma, Left Breast, Germline BRCA2 Pathogenic Variant
REASON FOR VISIT: Routine surveillance; completion of 1-year adjuvant olaparib

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okafor presents today for a scheduled follow-up visit coinciding with completion of her 52-week course of adjuvant olaparib (Lynparza). She has no new complaints and reports feeling well overall.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okafor is a 50-year-old woman with a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.S1982Rfs*22) who was diagnosed approximately two years ago with Stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, grade 3, with lymphovascular invasion, TILs 35%, PD-L1 CPS 18, and an HRD score of 68. She was treated on a KEYNOTE-522–based neoadjuvant regimen consisting of dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide (ddAC) x 4 cycles concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W, followed by weekly paclitaxel x 12 weeks with pembrolizumab. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by grade 2 autoimmune thyroiditis (pembrolizumab held for one cycle; subsequently resolved on levothyroxine replacement) and grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (paclitaxel dose-reduced to 80% from week 7).

She subsequently underwent left modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (levels I–II) and right prophylactic mastectomy with bilateral immediate tissue expander reconstruction. Surgical pathology demonstrated non-pCR with residual invasive ductal carcinoma measuring 1.8 cm (ypT1c), 3/16 positive axillary nodes (ypN1a), and an RCB index of 2.8 (RCB Class III), indicating extensive residual disease. She then received adjuvant capecitabine (cape) 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1–14 Q21 days for 8 cycles, concurrent with pembrolizumab to complete approximately 1 year of total checkpoint inhibitor exposure. Post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) was delivered to the left chest wall and regional nodal basins (total 60 Gy to chest wall with boost, 50 Gy to nodes). Cape was complicated by grade 2 hand-foot syndrome requiring one dose reduction to 75%; symptoms eventually improved to grade 1. Pembrolizumab was permanently discontinued after grade 3 immune-related hepatitis at adjuvant cycle 14 (ALT 412 U/L, AST 389 U/L), which resolved with high-dose steroids over approximately 6 weeks.

Following completion of capecitabine and steroid taper, she initiated adjuvant olaparib 300 mg BID per the OlympiA protocol, given her germline BRCA2 status and high-risk residual disease. Olaparib was started approximately one month after prednisone taper completion and normalization of liver enzymes. Her olaparib course was complicated by grade 2 anemia at week 8 (hemoglobin nadir 8.9 g/dL) that was managed with a single infusion of ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg IV without dose reduction; hemoglobin recovered to 11.1 g/dL and has been stable since. She completed her 52nd and final week of olaparib at her last visit.

She also underwent bilateral tissue expander exchange to permanent silicone implants (Mentor MemoryGel) earlier this year without complications. She has been back to full-time work as a high school biology teacher and describes her energy and functional status as excellent.

Surveillance imaging most recently performed at 12 months post-surgery (during olaparib) showed no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. A small 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule was incidentally identified at the 18-month CT but has not yet been performed at this time — per discussion, her next surveillance CT is due at 24 months post-surgery and will be ordered today.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Denies new fevers, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Energy level good, better than during active therapy.
- Breast/Chest: No new chest wall pain, skin changes, or palpable abnormalities. Bilateral implants feel comfortable. Denies nipple discharge (bilateral mastectomy).
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea on vigorous exercise; no resting dyspnea, hemoptysis, or pleuritis.
- GI: No nausea, emesis, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Appetite normal. Denies jaundice.
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding. Denies significant fatigue at current baseline.
- Neurologic: Persistent mild tingling in bilateral feet and fingertips, unchanged, grade 1. Managed with duloxetine. No falls or functional limitation.
- Endocrine: No palpitations, heat intolerance, or cold intolerance. Takes levothyroxine daily without missed doses.
- Gynecologic: Menstrual cycles irregular since chemotherapy, approximately every 5–7 weeks. Has met with gynecologic oncology regarding risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRBSO); scheduling in progress.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild joint stiffness in the mornings, resolves within 30 minutes. No new bone pain.
- Skin: Bilateral palms and soles appear normal. Residual faint radiation-induced skin changes at the left chest wall (hyperpigmentation), stable.
- Psych/Social: Mood overall good. Reports some situational anxiety around surveillance imaging but manageable. Sleeping adequately.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (immune-related thyroiditis, hypothyroid phase; dose pending TSH result today)
2. Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg PO daily (chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy)
3. Olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID — COMPLETING TODAY (final week)
4. Vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily
5. Calcium carbonate 500 mg BID

ALLERGIES: NKDA

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 14, Temperature 98.2°F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 72.1 kg, Height 162 cm, BMI 27.5.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Calm and engaged in discussion.

Head/Neck: Normocephalic. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged or tender to palpation.

Chest/Breast: Bilateral mastectomy with permanent silicone implants in place. No overlying skin erythema, warmth, capsular contracture, or rippling appreciated. No palpable masses in the bilateral chest wall. Left chest wall: mild radiation-induced hyperpigmentation along the radiation field, no moist desquamation, no induration suggestive of local recurrence. Left axillary basin: soft, no palpable adenopathy. Right axilla: no adenopathy.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No upper extremity lymphedema. Mild bilateral lower extremity trace edema. Bilateral upper and lower extremity neurologic exam: decreased light-touch sensation in distal digits and plantar surfaces bilaterally, consistent with grade 1 peripheral neuropathy. Strength 5/5 throughout.

Skin: Bilateral palms and soles without erythema, blistering, or desquamation. Radiation field changes at left chest wall as described above.

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):
- CBC: WBC 5.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.8 g/dL, MCV 91 fL, Plt 198 x10^9/L — within acceptable range, mild normochromic anemia, stable
- CMP: Na 139, K 4.0, Cr 0.78 mg/dL, BUN 14 — all normal
- LFTs: ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, Alk Phos 72 U/L, Total Bili 0.6 mg/dL — all within normal limits
- TSH: 5.1 mIU/L (mildly elevated, consistent with slight under-replacement; will uptitrate levothyroxine)
- Free T4: 0.9 ng/dL (low-normal)

IMAGING RESULTS:
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (12 months post-surgery, during olaparib): No evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant disease. Bilateral implants intact. Post-PMRT left chest wall changes stable. No hepatic or pulmonary lesions. Next surveillance CT due at approximately 18–24 months from surgery — being ordered today.

ASSESSMENT:
1. Stage IIB TNBC (ypT1cN1a), germline BRCA2-positive, RCB Class III — currently with no clinical or radiographic evidence of recurrence. Completed full 52-week course of adjuvant olaparib 300 mg BID as of this visit. One-year milestone reached. This is a high-risk patient by virtue of her residual nodal disease and RCB Class III, and ongoing vigilance with surveillance is essential.

2. Olaparib-associated anemia — resolved. Final CBC today demonstrates Hgb 11.8 g/dL, stable. No further intervention required. Hemoglobin may continue to trend upward off therapy.

3. Immune-related hypothyroidism (pembrolizumab-associated autoimmune thyroiditis) — TSH mildly elevated at 5.1 mIU/L today, consistent with mild under-replacement. Levothyroxine dose to be increased from 75 to 88 mcg daily. Recheck TFTs in 6–8 weeks.

4. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, grade 1 — chronic but stable. Duloxetine 30 mg daily continued. No functional impairment.

5. Irregular menstrual cycles (chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea, partially recovered) with germline BRCA2 status — risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRBSO) being coordinated with gynecologic oncology. Patient is appropriately counseled that premenopausal RRBSO will confer ovarian cancer risk reduction and may have downstream implications for cardiovascular and bone health; HRT options post-oophorectomy will be addressed by gyn-onc.

6. Breast reconstruction — bilateral tissue expander exchange to permanent silicone implants completed. No capsular contracture or implant complications on today's exam. Patient highly satisfied.

PLAN:
1. OLAPARIB: Complete final week; discontinue after today's confirmation of course completion. Congratulations conveyed to patient on completing a highly demanding multimodality treatment course.

2. SURVEILLANCE IMAGING: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (18–24 month post-surgery surveillance). Patient instructed to call with any new symptoms — bone pain, respiratory symptoms, or unexplained weight loss — before next scheduled scan.

3. LEVOTHYROXINE: Uptitrate from 75 mcg to 88 mcg daily. Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6–8 weeks; results to be communicated via patient portal.

4. DULOXETINE: Continue 30 mg daily for neuropathy. No change.

5. RRBSO: Awaiting final scheduling from gynecologic oncology. Patient reminded to follow up if scheduling is delayed beyond 3 months. Discussion regarding HRT use post-oophorectomy deferred to gyn-onc team; I reassured patient that short-term hormonal replacement for surgical menopause symptoms is generally considered reasonable in TNBC (ER-negative disease) though this warrants individualized discussion.

6. BONE HEALTH: Given anticipated premenopausal oophorectomy and prior chemotherapy, will check baseline DEXA scan. Patient instructed to continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

7. FOLLOW-UP: Return to medical oncology clinic in 3 months, or sooner if clinical concerns arise. Annual surveillance visits thereafter coordinated with breast surgery, genetics, and gynecologic oncology.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL: Patient has good insight and coping. Offered referral to survivorship clinic; patient interested — referral placed.

Time spent: 45 minutes, more than half of which in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending, Medical Oncology — Breast
